findings.issue: The refresh_token is discarded in both OAuth completion flows. HandoffRedeemResponse includes refresh_token: string (oauth.ts:14), but InvitationCompletePage destructures only { access_token } (line 57) and OAuthCallbackPage does the same (AppRouter.tsx:142). The token is never stored in localStorage or sessionStorage. ProfileProvider.tsx:58-62 confirms there is no refresh logic: on a 401 response it removes the access_token and signs the user out. This means every time the access_token expires, the user must re-authenticate through the full OAuth flow. The workstream introduced InvitationCompletePage which replicates this discard pattern from the pre-existing OAuthCallbackPage. All test mocks include refresh_token: 'refresh-123' but no test asserts it is stored or used, so the gap is invisible to the test suite.
findings.remediation: Store the refresh_token alongside access_token (e.g. localStorage.setItem('refresh_token', refresh_token)) and implement a 401-retry interceptor in makeRequest that uses the refresh_token to obtain a new access_token before failing the original request.

findings.issue: AbortSignal was plumbed through oauthApi.init's signature (oauth.ts:22) as part of the R13/R14 work, but neither call site actually passes a signal. AppRouter.tsx:50 calls initOAuth(provider, callbackUrl, challenge) with no 4th argument, and InvitationPage.tsx:52 calls initOAuth(provider, returnTo, challenge) with no 4th argument. This means the OAuth init request can only be cancelled by makeRequest's 30s timeout, not by component unmount or navigation. This is inconsistent with redeemOAuth, whose call sites (InvitationCompletePage.tsx:61, AppRouter.tsx:142) do pass an AbortSignal. If the user navigates away while initOAuth is in flight, the fetch resolves and window.location.assign(result.authorize_url) fires, unexpectedly redirecting the user from wherever they navigated to.
findings.remediation: Create an AbortController in handleOAuthLogin (both LoginPage and InvitationPage), pass its signal to initOAuth, and abort it on component unmount via a ref + useEffect cleanup, mirroring the pattern already used for redeemOAuth.

findings.issue: InvitationCompletePage tests for the 'verifier missing' and 'token missing' early-return error paths do not assert sessionStorage cleanup. The 'verifier missing' test (line 75-87) sets invitation_token in sessionStorage but only asserts the error message — it does not verify clearInvitationToken() ran (InvitationCompletePage.tsx:47). The 'token missing' test (line 89-107) sets oauth_verifier but does not verify clearVerifier() ran (InvitationCompletePage.tsx:53-54). If either cleanup call is removed, these tests still pass, leaving stale PKCE material in sessionStorage — the exact class of bug the workstream was meant to prevent. By contrast, the redeem-failure and accept-failure tests (lines 109-140) DO assert sessionStorage cleanup.
findings.remediation: Add expect(sessionStorage.getItem('invitation_token')).toBeNull() to the 'verifier missing' test and expect(sessionStorage.getItem('oauth_verifier')).toBeNull() to the 'token missing' test.

findings.issue: InvitationCompletePage success redirect target does not match the displayed message. The success card shows 'Redirecting to {orgSlug}...' (line 111) using result.organization_slug, but the actual redirect (line 79-80) navigates to the bare appBase root (window.location.assign(`${appBase}`)). The orgSlug is used only for display, not for constructing the redirect URL. A user who just joined 'test-org' sees 'Redirecting to test-org...' but is sent to the app root, which RootRedirect routes to /nodes — a generic page unrelated to the organization they just joined. If the app is multi-tenant and /nodes requires org context, the user may land in the wrong scope.
findings.remediation: Redirect to an org-specific URL such as `${appBase}/orgs/${result.organization_slug}` or `${appBase}/?org=${result.organization_slug}`, or change the success message to not reference the org slug if the root redirect is intentional.
